title: Pay up or not? Ransomware surge has victims facing tough choices.
gist: AI hacking tools drove confirmed ransomware victims up 389% year-on-year in 2025 (from ~1,600 to 7,831), commoditizing attacks so a lone actor can do nation-state-grade damage; nearly half of targets pay, and proposed public-sector payment bans threaten to spike cyber-insurance premiums and push criminals toward the unregulated private sector.

insights.claim: Confirmed ransomware victims rose 389% year-on-year in 2025, from around 1,600 in 2024 to 7,831 globally, according to Fortinet's Dave Spillane, powered by malicious AI tools like WormGPT, FraudGPT, and BruteForceAI.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: A near-5x one-year jump in victims quantifies the AI-driven attack surge—bullish structural demand for cybersecurity spend and defensive tooling.
insights.quote: confirmed ransomware victims rose 389 percent year-on-year in 2025, from around 1,600 in 2024 to 7,831 globally.
insights.locator: This has been powered by the rise of malicious AI hacking tools

insights.claim: AI lets attackers target four organizations simultaneously in the time one attack previously took, sharply lowering attacker cost per attack while defense costs rise.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: The widening cost-to-attack vs cost-to-defend asymmetry favors sustained secular growth in security budgets and cyber-insurance repricing.
insights.quote: In the time it would have previously taken to commit one ransomware attack, hackers can now target four separate organizations simultaneously.
insights.locator: “In the time it would have previously taken

insights.claim: AI now lets individuals with 'half-baked skills' accomplish attacks that previously required nation-states, per Nile CMO Shashi Kiran.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Democratized attack capability broadens the threat actor pool, raising baseline risk for every SMB and enterprise.
insights.quote: What required nation states earlier can be accomplished by individuals with half-baked skills leveraging the power of AI.
insights.locator: “The cost per attack has dramatically decreased

themes.theme: Payment bans and the insurance knock-on

insights.claim: The UK government is advancing plans to prohibit public-sector bodies and critical national infrastructure—including the NHS, local councils, and schools—from paying ransoms.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Regulatory payment bans reshape incident economics and could redirect attacker targeting—watch which jurisdictions follow.
insights.locator: Globally, some jurisdictions are responding by banning payments

insights.claim: If public bodies are banned from paying, the cyber-insurance market will exclude such payouts and drive premiums 'sky-high,' per Risk Ledger CEO Haydn Brooks, who also warns criminals will 'aggressively pivot' to the unregulated private sector.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Payment bans could reprice cyber insurance and shift attack volume to private firms—relevant to insurers and mid-market corporates.
insights.quote: excluding these payouts and driving premiums sky-high as the costs dwarf the original ransom demands.
insights.locator: If public bodies are banned from paying

insights.claim: Statewide payment bans in North Carolina (2021) and Florida (2022) do not appear to have materially deterred criminal activity, per DriveSavers' Andy Maus.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Empirical evidence that bans don't deter attacks undercuts the policy case and argues for exposure-reduction over prohibition.
insights.quote: neither ban appears to have materially deterred criminal activity.
insights.locator: He notes that in North Carolina and Florida

insights.claim: Paying ransoms does not guarantee recovery because threat actors cannot be trusted to delete data, and re-extortion plus ongoing monetization of stolen data are commonplace, per SentinelOne's Jim Walter.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Undermines the ROI case for paying and reinforces prevention/backup investment as the rational allocation.
insights.quote: Paying absolutely does not guarantee recovery, it actually encourages further crime and extortion.

insights.claim: A growing market of ransom negotiators, incident response teams, and breach coaches has emerged to help companies assess data-recovery options during attacks.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Signals an expanding services niche around incident response—an investable adjacency to core security software.
insights.locator: There is now a growing market of services

insights.claim: The more important lever is making ransomware less profitable via 'exposure management,' since most attacks still exploit known vulnerabilities, exposed systems, and security gaps, per Tenable's Gavin Millard.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Points investment toward exposure management, MFA enforcement, and least-privilege access controls as the highest-leverage defenses.
insights.quote: the more important question is how to make ransomware less profitable in the first place
insights.locator: But instead of whether to ban payments or not
